headline: Mertz's arm carries Miami, but Washington's ground game proves decisive
body: Graham Mertz had no answer when asked why his 389 passing yards and three touchdowns weren't enough to get Miami over the hump in a 35-28 loss to Washington. The Hurricanes quarterback was sharp and decisive all night, completing 30 of 53 attempts and finding Campen for eight receptions and 89 yards. Mertz's performance was the kind that ordinarily wins games on the road, but it ran headlong into a Washington team that understood what Miami couldn't seem to execute: the value of feeding a back repeatedly until the defense breaks.

Washington's rushing attack accumulated just 93 yards on 11 carries, hardly a dominant showing on paper. But that number masks the real story. The Huskies controlled the clock and the line of scrimmage when it mattered most, allowing their defense to stay fresh and their passing game to operate in rhythm. Graham Brown needed just 13 completions on 17 attempts for 138 yards because Washington never got behind. When the Huskies needed a touchdown in the air, WR James took a slant 103 yards worth of impact, breaking tackles and moving the chains when scores were tight.

Miami's fumble late didn't cost them the game outright, but it crystallized a broader issue: the Hurricanes lived by the air and, ultimately, fell short by it. A team that generates 404 yards of offense and scores 28 points should win more often than not. Yet in a game where one team will take what the defense gives them and the other keeps pressing for more, Washington's restraint and execution in critical moments separated the two.
turningPoint: Washington's ability to sustain drives with the ground game despite modest rushing totals kept Miami's talented defense on the field long enough to wear them down and prevented the Hurricanes from capitalizing on their passing efficiency.

unsungHero.name: Clemons
unsungHero.why: Clemons' 12 tackles and disruptive sack set the tone for Washington's defensive resilience without the flashiness of a pick or fumble recovery, keeping Miami's high-powered passing attack consistently off balance.
